questions.text: This cell which is the longest in the human body:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Muscle cells, also known as muscle fibers, are responsible for the contraction and movement of muscles in the body. While muscle cells can vary in length depending on the muscle they belong to, they are generally not as long as nerve cells. Muscle fibers are elongated cells that can span from a few millimeters to several centimeters in length. They contain specialized structures called myofibrils, which are responsible for the contractile properties of muscle tissue.

Nerve cells, or neurons, are specialized cells of the nervous system that transmit electrical signals throughout the body. Neurons consist of a cell body, dendrites (short, branched extensions that receive signals from other neurons), and an elongated projection called an axon. It is the axon that can be exceptionally long, allowing for communication over long distances. For example, some neurons in the spinal cord extend all the way to the toes or fingertips, making them the longest cells in the human body.

Bone cells, also known as osteocytes, are the primary cells found in bone tissue. While bone cells play a vital role in maintaining bone health and remodeling, they are not typically characterized by their length. Bone tissue itself can be quite long, as it forms the structural framework of the skeletal system, but individual bone cells do not exhibit significant elongation compared to nerve cells.

Gland cells are found in various glands of the body and are responsible for producing and secreting substances such as hormones, enzymes, or mucus. Gland cells can have different shapes and sizes depending on the gland they belong to, but they are generally not known for their exceptional length. Their function primarily involves the production and release of specific substances rather than elongated structures.

questions.explanation: The nerve cells or neurons are the longest cells in the human body, with some neurons being as long as 1-1.5 meters. These cells are responsible for transmitting signals throughout the body. It is the axon that can be exceptionally long, allowing for communication over long distances. For example, some neurons in the spinal cord extend all the way to the toes or fingertips, making them the longest cells in the human body.

The sensory input from the tendon stretches receptors (muscle spindles), and the motor output leads to a quick contraction of the calf muscle, causing the foot to move.

The knee jerk, also known as the patellar reflex, is not a superficial reflex. Like the ankle jerk, it is a deep tendon reflex. It is elicited by tapping the patellar tendon just below the kneecap, which stretches the quadriceps muscle. The reflexive response causes the knee to jerk forward due to the contraction of the quadriceps muscle.

questions.text: The stretch reflex, the Golgi tendon reflex, the crossed extensor reflex, and the withdrawal reflex are included in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The stretch reflex is a type of spinal reflex that occurs when a muscle is stretched. It is a monosynaptic reflex, meaning it involves only one synapse in the spinal cord. The sensory neuron (also known as the muscle spindle) detects the stretching of the muscle and directly stimulates the motor neuron to contract the same muscle, leading to its quick response. The stretch reflex helps maintain muscle tone and provides a rapid response to changes in muscle length.

Spinal reflexes are reflex actions that occur entirely within the spinal cord without involving the brain. They are rapid and involuntary responses to specific stimuli, and they play a crucial role in maintaining posture, balance, and protective responses to potentially harmful stimuli. Examples of spinal reflexes include the stretch reflex, withdrawal reflex, and crossed extensor reflex.

The Golgi tendon reflex is also a type of spinal reflex. It operates on a different principle than the stretch reflex. It is a protective reflex that prevents muscles from exerting too much force and potentially damaging tendons. When a muscle contracts and pulls on its tendon, Golgi tendon organs (located at the junction of the muscle and tendon) detect the tension. If the tension becomes too high, the Golgi tendon organ inhibits the motor neuron, leading to relaxation of the muscle and reducing tension on the tendon.

The crossed extensor reflex is a type of spinal reflex that accompanies the withdrawal reflex. When a painful stimulus is applied to one side of the body, such as stepping on a sharp object, the withdrawal reflex occurs, causing the limb to pull away from the pain. At the same time, the crossed extensor reflex occurs on the opposite side of the body. It involves the activation of extensor muscles in the opposite limb, allowing the body to maintain balance and support while the other limb is withdrawn.

questions.explanation: The spinal reflex includes the stretch reflex, the Golgi tendon reflex, the crossed extensor reflex, and the withdrawal reflex.

questions.text: Nerve cells transmit messages faster when they have:

Non-myelinated axons are axons that lack a myelin sheath, a fatty insulating layer that wraps around some axons. Without myelin, the speed of message transmission is slower compared to myelinated axons.

Myelinated axons are axons that are surrounded by a myelin sheath, a fatty insulating layer. The myelin sheath acts as an electrical insulator, allowing nerve impulses to "jump" between gaps in the myelin called "Nodes of Ranvier." This phenomenon, known as saltatory conduction, speeds up the transmission of nerve impulses significantly. As a result, myelinated axons can transmit messages much faster than non-myelinated axons.

options.explanation: Relay neurons, also known as interneurons or association neurons, are found entirely within the CNS and act as intermediaries between sensory neurons and motor neurons. Sensory neurons, also called afferent neurons, are responsible for transmitting sensory information from sensory receptors (such as eyes, ears, hands, and feet) to the central nervous system. These neurons detect various stimuli, such as light, sound, touch, temperature, and pain, and convert them into electrical signals called action potentials.

A mixed neuron is a type of sensory neuron that carries both sensory and motor information. The axon transmits signals away from the cell body, while the dendrite receives signals and carries them toward the cell body.

options.explanation: The contraction of hair muscles, also known as arrector pili muscles, is primarily regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. It causes hair to stand on end, a response known as piloerection.

Sweat gland activity is mainly regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. The sympathetic system promotes the production and release of sweat in response to heat or stress, not the parasympathetic system.
The acceleration of the heart rate is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system. It triggers the "fight or flight" response, increasing heart rate to prepare the body for action.

This is the correct answer. The parasympathetic nervous system is responsible for constricting the pupil (making it smaller) in response to non-stressful, restful situations. This action allows for better near vision and reduces the amount of light entering the eye.

questions.explanation: The action of the parasympathetic nervous system is opposite to that of the sympathetic nervous system. If the sympathetic nervous system accelerates an action, the parasympathetic nervous system slows it. However, neither system is exclusively excitatory or inhibitory. The parasympathetic fibres constrict the pupil, decrease the rate and force of heartbeat, dilate many blood vessels, lower the arterial blood pressure, quicken the peristaltic movements, and contract the urinary bladder.

(2)The photopigments are embedded in the membrane discs of the inner segment. (3)Retinal is a derivative of vitamin A. (4)Retinal is a light-absorbing part of all the visual photopigments.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a hallucinogenic drug?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Caffeine has a chemical formula C8H10N4O2 and belongs to the xanthine chemical class.
This option is incorrect because Morphine is a natural alkaloid and powerful opioid analgesic (pain-relieving) drug derived from the opium poppy plant (Papaver somniferum).
This option is correct because Lysergic acid diethylamide, commonly known as LSD, is a potent hallucinogenic drug.

This option is incorrect because Opium is a highly addictive and potent narcotic drug derived from the latex sap of the opium poppy plant, known scientifically as Papaver somniferum. Opium has been used for centuries for its analgesic (pain-relieving) and euphoric effects.

questions.explanation: LSD is a psychedelic drug since it causes optical and auditory hallucinations and induces behavioral abnormalities. Opium and morphine are opiate narcotics that suppress brain activity and relieve pain. Caffeine is a stimulant that temporarily stimulates the nervous system. Lysergic acid diethylamide, commonly known as LSD, is a potent hallucinogenic drug.

questions.text: Select the correct statement from the ones given below:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Barbiturates, when given to criminals, make them tell the truth
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the idea that barbiturates, or any specific class of drugs, can be used to make criminals or individuals tell the truth is a common misconception perpetuated by popular culture but is not supported by scientific or medical evidence.

options.text: Morphine is often given to person who have undergone surgery, as a pain killer
options.explanation: This option is correct because Morphine is commonly administered to individuals who have undergone surgery as a painkiller and to manage post-operative pain. Morphine is an opioid analgesic, and it is highly effective at relieving severe pain.

options.text: Chewing tobacco lowers blood pressure and heart rate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because, Chewing tobacco does not lower blood pressure and heart rate. In fact, the use of smokeless tobacco products, including chewing tobacco, is associated with several negative health effects.

options.text: Cocaine is given to patients after surgery as it stimulates recovery
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Cocaine is not used as a medication to stimulate recovery after surgery. Cocaine is a powerful stimulant drug with a high potential for abuse and addiction.

questions.explanation: Morphine is a potent opioid analgesic used mainly to relieve severe and persistent pain, particularly in terminally ill patients or who have undergone surgery. It also induces feelings of euphoria. It is administered by mouth, injection, or in suppositories. Common side effects ar nausea and vomiting, constipation, and drowsiness. With regular use, tolerance develops and dependence may occur.

questions.text: It is not true about cell body of neuron:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cell body of a neuron serves as the main nutritional part, where essential biosynthesis occurs. It contains organelles that support the neuron's metabolic functions.
Axoplasm refers specifically to the cytoplasm found within an axon. The cell body does not contain axoplasm; instead, it includes the nucleus and other organelles vital for the neuron's function.
The cell body is indeed involved in the biosynthesis of materials required for neuronal function and regeneration, making this statement accurate.
The cell body can support the regeneration of axonal and dendritic fibers if it remains intact, which is a true statement regarding neuronal repair capabilities.

questions.explanation: The cell body contains the nucleus, mitochondra, Golgi apparatus, and organelles for metabolism and biosynthasis, but axoplasm is specific to axons, not the soma.

questions.text: Which of the following is most abundant type of neurons in CNS?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons are specialized for transmitting information from sensory organs to the CNS. They are actually the least abundant type of neuron in the CNS, accounting for approximately 10% of the total neuronal population.

Also known as interneurons, associative neurons are the most abundant neurons in the CNS. They facilitate communication between sensory and motor neurons and are integral to complex functions such as learning, memory, and decision-making.
Motor neurons transmit signals from the CNS to muscles, enabling movement. They are significantly less abundant than interneurons, constituting about 1% of the total neuron population in the CNS.

Neuroglial cells are supportive cells in the CNS, not neurons. While they outnumber neurons by about 10 to 1, they do not directly participate in neuronal signaling, so they cannot be classified as neurons.

questions.explanation: Interneurons (associative neurons) are the most abundant. neurons in the CNS. They process and relay information between sensory and motor neurons and are crucial for higher-order functions like learning, memory, and decision-making.

questions.text: These are the structures that respond when they are stimulated by an impulse coming through motor neuron:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glandular cells respond to impulses from the autonomic nervous system because it is an effector, so they secrete hormones as its action.

Thermoreceptors respond to changes in temperature. They are located in the skin and in the internal organs. Thermoreceptors send signals to the brain, which tells the body how to regulate its temperature.
Sensory neurons are responsible for sending information from the body to the brain. They do not respond to impulses from motor neurons.

Pacinian corpuscles are a type of sensory receptor that responds to pressure. They are located in the skin and in the internal organs. Pacinian corpuscles send signals to the brain, which tells the body how to respond to pressure.

questions.explanation: Glandular cells respond to impulses from the autonomic nervous system. The autonomic nervous system acts on glands as it is an effector, so it produces hormones and secretions that act on the specific target organs.

questions.text: These contain cell bodies of neurons:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: cell bodies of neurons are found in the gray matter of the central nervous system (CNS) and the ganglia of the peripheral nervous system (PNS).

The cell bodies of neurons are found in the gray matter of the central nervous system (CNS) and the ganglia of the peripheral nervous system (PNS). In the CNS, the cell bodies of neurons are found in the cerebral cortex, the thalamus, the hypothalamus, the brainstem, and the spinal cord. In the PNS, the cell bodies of neurons are found in the ganglia, which are clusters of neurons located outside the CNS.
The cell bodies of neurons are found in the gray matter of the central nervous system (CNS) and the ganglia of the peripheral nervous system (PNS).
The cell bodies of neurons are found in the gray matter of the central nervous system (CNS) and the ganglia of the peripheral nervous system (PNS).

questions.explanation: The cell bodies of neurons are found in the gray matter of the central nervous system (CNS) and the ganglia of the peripheral nervous system (PNS). In the CNS, the cell bodies of neurons are found in the cerebral cortex, the thalamus, the hypothalamus, the brainstem, and the spinal cord. In the PNS, the cell bodies of neurons are found in the ganglia, which are clusters of neurons located outside the CNS.

questions.text: The 24 hour (diurnal) rhythm of our body such as the sleep­wake cycle is regulated by which hormone?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Calcitonin is a hormone that your thyroid gland makes and releases to help regulate calcium levels in your blood by decreasing them.
Prolactin is a polypeptide hormone that is responsible for lactation, breast development, and hundreds of other actions needed to maintain homeostasis.

Adrenaline, also known as epinephrine, is a hormone and medication which is involved in regulating visceral functions. It appears as a white microcrystalline granule. Adrenaline is normally produced by the adrenal glands and by a small number of neurons in the medulla oblongata.

Melatonin is a hormone secreted by the pineal gland and retina of vertebrates. Melatonin secretion by the pineal gland is linked to the dark-light cycle of the organism’s environment, being greatest at night and lowest by day. The hormone is involved in regulating certain diurnal and seasonal changes in the body, such as the reproductive cycle in seasonally breeding animals. It is used as a drug to treat sleep disorders and symptoms of jet lag.

questions.explanation: Melatonin is a hormone secreted by the pineal gland and retina of vertebrates. Melatonin secretion by the pineal gland is linked to the dark-light cycle of the organism’s environment, being greatest at night and lowest by day. The hormone is involved in regulating certain diurnal and seasonal changes in the body, such as the reproductive cycle in seasonally breeding animals. It is used as a drug to treat sleep disorders and symptoms of jet lag.

questions.text: Motor neurons have:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Motor neurons, which are responsible for transmitting signals from the nervous system to muscles and glands, typically have long axons that extend from the spinal cord or brain to their target tissues. These axons can be quite long, reaching all the way to the muscles they innervate. Therefore, motor neurons do not usually have short axons.

Motor neurons indeed have long axons. These axons enable motor neurons to transmit signals over relatively long distances to the target muscles or glands they control. This feature allows for efficient and rapid communication between the nervous system and the muscles they stimulate.

Dendrites are branched extensions of neurons that receive incoming signals from other neurons and transmit those signals to the cell body. Motor neurons typically have relatively short dendrites compared to their long axons. The dendrites of motor neurons receive signals from other neurons, including sensory neurons and interneurons, which then get integrated in the motor neuron's cell body and translated into motor commands.
The one which carry impulse towards cell body is called dendron, if it is a single fibre . But dendron is present only in sensory neurons, whereas, motor neurons have dendrites.

questions.explanation: Motor neurons indeed have long axons. These axons enable motor neurons to transmit signals over relatively long distances to the target muscles or glands they control. This feature allows for efficient and rapid communication between the nervous system and the muscles they stimulate.

questions.text: Other names for associative neurons are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not a commonly used term, but it essentially refers to the same type of neuron as "associative neurons". They help transmit signals between different neurons in the brain and spinal cord.

Motor neurons are responsible for carrying signals from the central nervous system (CNS) to muscles or glands, resulting in specific actions or responses. They are involved in controlling movements and other motor functions.

Associative neurons are commonly known by several names, and one of them is "Intermediate neurons". These neurons act as connectors or intermediators between sensory neurons (afferent neurons) and motor neurons (efferent neurons) in the nervous system.

These are neurons that release neurotransmitters to stimulate or inhibit the activity of another neuron across a synapse. They are located before the synapse, while postsynaptic neurons are located after the synapse.

questions.explanation: Associative neurons are commonly known by several names, and one of them is "Intermediate neurons". These neurons act as connectors or intermediators between sensory neurons (afferent neurons) and motor neurons (efferent neurons) in the nervous system. As the most abundant type of neuron, interneurons are crucial for integrating sensory information, processing complex neural circuits, and regulating motor activity.

questions.text: The longest cell in the body of an animal is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Osteocytes are relatively small cells. They are mature bone cells that originate from osteoblasts and are responsible for maintaining the bone matrix.
Chromatophores are among the larger cell types due to their function, but they are not larger than neurons. They are actually, pigment-containing and light-generating cells.
The longest cells in the human body are neurons, specifically the motor neurons. These cells can have axons that extend from the base of the spinal cord to the toes.
They are relatively small as compared to other types of cells in animal's body.

questions.explanation: The longest cells in the human body are neurons, specifically the motor neurons. These cells can have axons that extend from the base of the spinal cord to the toes.

questions.text: The main transmitter for synapses that lie outside the central nervous system is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adrenaline is a hormone and neurotransmitter that is primarily released by the adrenal glands in response to stress or as part of the "fight or flight" response. While adrenaline can act as a neurotransmitter in certain situations, it is not the main neurotransmitter for synapses outside the CNS.

Serotonin is a neurotransmitter found in the central nervous system and is involved in regulating mood, appetite, sleep, and other functions. It is not the primary neurotransmitter for synapses outside the CNS.

Dopamine is another neurotransmitter primarily found in the central nervous system, and it plays a significant role in reward and pleasure pathways, motivation, and motor control. Dopamine is not the main neurotransmitter for synapses outside the CNS.

This is the correct answer. Acetylcholine is the main neurotransmitter for synapses that lie outside the central nervous system (PNS). It is involved in transmitting signals from motor neurons to muscles, allowing for voluntary muscle contractions, and is also involved in various autonomic functions in the PNS.

questions.explanation: The main neurotransmitter for synapses that lie outside the central nervous system is Acetylcholine. Acetylcholine (ACh) is a neurotransmitter that is commonly found in the peripheral nervous system (PNS) and is responsible for transmitting signals from neurons to muscles, glands, and other target cells outside the CNS.

questions.text: All of the following about reflex action are true except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is false. Reflex actions are not voluntary; they occur without conscious effort or control.
This statement is true. Reflex actions are indeed involuntary, as they are automatic responses to specific stimuli.

options.text: It is found in higher animals.
options.explanation: This statement is true. Reflex actions are found in higher animals, including mammals, birds, and reptiles, as well as in simpler animals like invertebrates. Reflexes are important survival mechanisms that allow animals to quickly respond to environmental cues and potential threats.
This statement is false. The correct option should be "b) It is involuntary," as reflex actions are involuntary responses and not voluntary actions.

questions.explanation: Reflex actions are involuntary responses to specific stimuli. When a sensory stimulus is detected, the body automatically generates a reflexive response without conscious thought or control. Reflex actions are rapid, automatic, and do not involve the decision-making centers of the brain.

questions.text: Stimulus of deep pressure is detected by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The stimulus of deep pressure is detected by pacinian corpuscles. Pacinian corpuscles are large, encapsulated mechanoreceptors that are found in the dermis and subcutaneous tissue. They are sensitive to vibration and deep pressure.
Krause end bulbs are not the correct answer because they are sensitive to light touch, not deep pressure.
Meissner's corpuscles are not the correct answer because they are sensitive to fine touch, not deep pressure.
Merkel's endings are not the correct answer because they are not mechanoreceptors, so they do not detect touch.

questions.explanation: The stimulus of deep pressure is detected by pacinian corpuscles. Pacinian corpuscles are large, encapsulated mechanoreceptors that are found in the dermis and subcutaneous tissue. They are sensitive to vibration and deep pressure.

questions.text: Receptors are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brain is not a receptor. It is the organ that receives signals from the receptors and interprets them.
Muscles and glands are not receptors either. They are organs that respond to signals from the receptors.
Receptors are sensory cells that detect stimuli from the environment and send signals to the brain. They are found in all parts of the body, including the skin, muscles, eyes, and nose.
Nerve carry nerve impulse from receptor to CNS and then back to effector.

questions.explanation: Receptors are sensory cells that detect stimuli from the environment and send signals to the brain. They are found in all parts of the body, including the skin, muscles, eyes, and nose.

questions.text: Motor neuron are multipolar but:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Motor neurons are a type of nerve cell, and they are classified as multipolar neurons due to their distinctive shape and structure. Multipolar neurons have multiple processes (extensions)emanating from the cell body. However, compared to other types of multipolar neurons, motor neurons are generally less branched.
Motor neurons have multiple dendrites, their dendritic branching pattern is generally less complex than some other types of multipolar neurons found in the brain, such as pyramidal cells.
All types of neurons have branches, therefore this will be wrong.
It can not be none as option A is correct.

questions.explanation: Motor neurons are a specific type of neurons that are responsible for transmitting nerve signals from the central nervous system (CNS) to muscle fibers, leading to muscle contraction and motor control. Motor neurons are indeed multipolar neurons, meaning they have multiple processes extending from the cell body. However, the dendrites of motor neurons are generally less branched compared to other types of multipolar neurons.

questions.text: Node of Ranvier are also known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The myelin sheath is a fatty, insulating layer that wraps around the axon of some neurons, forming a protective covering. However, there are periodic interruptions in the myelin sheath, which expose small sections of the axon called nodes of Ranvier. These nodes are not covered by myelin and allow for the rapid and efficient propagation of nerve impulses.

Node of Ranvier is also known as "myelin sheath gap" or "neurofibril node." These terms refer to the small gaps or spaces that occur at regular intervals along the axon of a myelinated neuron. The node of Ranvier is an essential feature of myelinated nerve fibers and plays a critical role in the efficient conduction of nerve impulses.

The primary structure involved in muscle contraction is the myofibril, which is a cylindrical organelle found within muscle cells (muscle fibers). Myofibrils are composed of repeating contractile units called sarcomeres, which contain actin and myosin filaments responsible for muscle contraction.
This is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The term "node of Ranvier" is named after Louis-Antoine Ranvier, a French histologist who first described these structures in the late 1800s. These specialized regions along the axon of a neuron have a high concentration of ion channels and play a crucial role in facilitating the rapid conduction of nerve impulses, also known as action potentials, along the length of the axon.

questions.text: In the mammalian eye, the ‘fovea’ is the center of the visual field where:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Rods are concentrated in the periphery of the retina, not the fovea.

options.text: More rods than cones are found
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The fovea has a high density of cones, not rods.

options.text: High density of cones occurs, but it has no rods
options.explanation: A small oval, yellowish area of the retina lying exactly opposite to the centre of the cornea is named the macula lutea or yellow spot, which has at its middle a shallow depression, the fovea centralis. The fovea centralis has cone cells only. It is devoid of rods and blood vessels. The fovea centralis is the place of the most distinct vision.

options.text: An equal number of rods and cones
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The fovea is solely composed of cones, with no rods present.

questions.explanation: The fovea is the part of the retina with the highest concentration of cones, responsible for sharp central vision and color perception. Since cones are primarily used for detailed vision in bright light, the fovea has no rods, which are responsible for vision in low light conditions.

questions.text: The sympathetic nervous system induces:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Secretion of digestive juices is induced by parasympathetic nervous systems in relaxed state unlike sympathetic nervous system.

Most ganglion fibres of the sympathetic system arise from the middle portion of the spinal cord and almost terminate in ganglia that lie near the cord. This system is important during emergency situations and is associated with “fight or flight.” This system accelerates the heart beat, dilates the pupil and inhibits the digestion of food etc.
The sympathetic nervous system causes the secretion of small amounts of thick saliva, while the parasympathetic nervous system produces large amounts of watery saliva. So this is partially incorrect.
This is incorrect as increased heart rate is a response to fight or flight which is induced by the sympathetic nervous system.

questions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system is a component of the autonomic nervous system consisting of a pair of sympathetic trunks, preganglionic sympathetic fibres, postganglionic sympathetic fibres and collateral ganglia. It quickens the rate and force of heartbeat while it inhibits the secretion of saliva and gastric juice. It prepares the body for fight or flight.

questions.text: Which of the following neurotransmitters affect sleep, mood and learning?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While dopamine is involved in mood and learning, epinephrine primarily regulates metabolism, attention, and the body's 'fight-or-flight' response, rather than sleep or mood.
Serotonin does affect mood and sleep, but norepinephrine is more related to alertness and stress responses rather than directly influencing mood or learning.

Both dopamine and serotonin are crucial for mood regulation, sleep, and learning. Serotonin helps produce melatonin, which affects the sleep-wake cycle, while dopamine is critical for learning and mood stabilization.
Both are mainly involved in the body's immediate stress response and do not directly influence mood, sleep, or learning in the same way as serotonin and dopamine.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Dopamine and serotonin. Both neurotransmitters play a significant role in mood regulation, the sleep-wake cycle, and learning processes. Dopamine is essential for learning and motivation, while serotonin is involved in mood stabilization and sleep regulation through melatonin production. The other options include neurotransmitters like epinephrine and norepinephrine, which are more associated with stress responses and alertness rather than directly influencing mood, sleep, or learning.

questions.text: It acts as a relay neuron:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons, also known as afferent neurons, are responsible for transmitting sensory information from sensory organs to the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) the relay neurons. These neurons detect external stimuli and convert them into electrical signals for further processing.

Relay neurons are responsible for relaying signals between sensory neurons and motor neurons or between other interneurons. They help transmit information within the central nervous system (CNS) and are involved in coordinating and integrating neural activity. Associative neurons are found exclusively in the central nervous system, so can act as relay neurons.

Motor neurons, also known as efferent neurons, carry signals from the central nervous system the relay neuron to muscles and glands. They are responsible for initiating and controlling muscle contractions and glandular secretions, enabling us to move and perform various bodily functions.
Unipolar neurons are primarily sensory neurons, carrying information from receptors to the CNS, not relaying between neurons.

questions.explanation: Relay neurons are responsible for relaying signals between sensory neurons and motor neurons or between other interneurons. They help transmit information within the central nervous system (CNS) and are involved in coordinating and integrating neural activity. Associative neurons are found exclusively in the central nervous system, so can act as relay neurons.

questions.text: Most reflex arcs are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A monosynaptic reflex involves only one synapse and typically consists of a direct connection between a sensory neuron and a motor neuron. The most well-known example of a monosynaptic reflex is the stretch reflex, where a muscle is rapidly stretched, and the sensory neuron activates the motor neuron to contract the muscle.

This is the correct answer. A polysynaptic reflex involves multiple synapses and interneurons between the sensory neuron and the motor neuron. Examples of polysynaptic reflexes include the withdrawal reflex and the crossed-extensor reflex.
There is no such term as "hemisynaptic reflex" in neurophysiology. It appears to be an incorrect option.

The correct answer is "b) Polysynaptic reflex." Most reflex arcs in the body involve multiple synapses and interneurons, making them polysynaptic reflexes. Monosynaptic reflexes are less common but still play essential roles in reflexive actions.

questions.explanation: Most reflex arcs are "b) Polysynaptic reflex." A polysynaptic reflex involves multiple synapses and interneurons between the sensory neuron and the motor neuron in the reflex arc. In this type of reflex, sensory information from the sensory neuron is transmitted to interneurons in the spinal cord or brainstem before reaching the motor neurons that initiate the appropriate response.

questions.text: Reflex action is controlled by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Reflex actions are involuntary actions that are controlled by the spinal cord, which is a part of the CNS.
Reflex actions are involuntary actions that are controlled by the spinal cord, which is a part of the CNS.

The whole mechanism of reflex action occurs in such a fashion that there is no conscious control of the brain. Stimulation occurs through the peripheral nervous system, and the response to this peripheral nerve stimulation is involuntary.

Reflex actions are not controlled by the circulatory system. The circulatory system, also known as the cardiovascular system, is responsible for the transport of blood, oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and waste products throughout the body. It consists of the heart, blood vessels, and blood.

questions.explanation: Reflex actions are involuntary actions that are controlled by the spinal cord, which is a part of the CNS. The spinal cord controls reflex actions by receiving signals from sensory neurons and processing them through a neural pathway called a reflex arc to motor neurons, which then trigger an immediate, involuntary response from muscles or glands. This process bypasses the brain, allowing for rapid reactions to stimuli like touching a hot object or the knee-jerk reflex, which is a classic example of a spinal-cord-controlled reflex.

questions.text: Reflex action is the simplest form of response in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Higher animals, including vertebrates like mammals, birds, and reptiles, also exhibit reflex actions. However, they also possess more complex neural networks and brain structures that enable them to exhibit a wide range of behaviors and cognitive processes beyond simple reflexive responses.
The size of an animal is not the primary determinant of whether it exhibits reflex actions or not. Reflex actions can be observed in animals of various sizes, from small insects to large mammals.
This is the correct answer. Reflex actions are most commonly observed in simpler animals with less complex nervous systems, such as invertebrates like worms, insects, and mollusks.

The term "lowest animals" is not a scientifically accurate classification, and it is not commonly used to describe organisms. The presence of reflex actions is not determined by the hierarchical classification of animals.

questions.explanation: Reflex actions are the simplest form of response and are commonly observed in "c) Simpler animals." These responses are rapid, automatic, and involuntary reactions to certain stimuli without involving conscious thought or decision-making processes. In simpler animals, such as invertebrates like worms, insects, and mollusks, reflex actions serve as essential survival mechanisms to quickly and efficiently respond to environmental cues and potential threats.

questions.text: Stretch receptors are present in_of the tetrapods.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hepatic arteries are not the correct answer because they are the arteries that supply blood to the liver. Stretch receptors are not typically found in the liver.

Stretch receptors are present in carotid arteries of tetrapods.These receptors are also known as baroreceptors. They are located in the walls of the carotid arteries, which are the major arteries that supply blood to the head. Stretch receptors are sensitive to changes in blood pressure.
Renal arteries are not the correct answer because they are the arteries that supply blood to the kidneys. Stretch receptors are not typically found in the kidneys.
Pulmonary arteries are not the correct answer because they are the arteries that supply blood to the lungs. Stretch receptors are not typically found in the lungs.

questions.explanation: Stretch receptors are present in the carotid arteries of tetrapods.These receptors are also known as baroreceptors. They are located in the walls of the carotid arteries, which are the major arteries that supply blood to the head. Stretch receptors are sensitive to changes in blood pressure.

questions.text: Stretch receptors are present in_of tetrapods.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Muscles only is not the correct answer because stretch receptors are also found in organs.
Organs only is not the correct answer because stretch receptors are also found in muscles.

Stretch receptors are present in both muscles and organs of tetrapods.They are also known as mechanoreceptors.They are sensitive to stretching and can be found in many different tissues, including muscles, tendons, joints, and organs.
Bone only is not the correct answer because stretch receptors are not found in bone.

questions.explanation: Stretch receptors are present in both muscles and organs of tetrapods.They are also known as mechanoreceptors.They are sensitive to stretching and can be found in many different tissues, including muscles, tendons, joints, and organs.

questions.text: Which types of the receptors are present in the ear?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chemoreceptors are sensory cells that respond to chemical stimuli. They are found in the nose, tongue, and other parts of the body. The ear does not have any chemoreceptors.
Photoreceptors are sensory cells that respond to light stimuli. They are found in the retina of the eye. The ear does not have any photoreceptors.
Thermoreceptors are sensory cells that respond to temperature stimuli. They are found in the skin and other parts of the body. The ear does not have any thermoreceptors.
Mechanoreceptors detect stimuli of touch, pressure, stretching, sound waves and motion. So mechanoreceptors are present in ear.

questions.explanation: Mechanoreceptors detect stimuli of touch, pressure, stretching, sound waves and motion. So mechanoreceptors are present in ear. They convert these physical forces into electrical signals that are sent to the nervous system, enabling sensations of touch, proprioception (body position), and the sense of hearing and balance. Examples include Merkel's disks, Meissner's corpuscles, Pacinian corpuscles, and Ruffini endings in the skin, as well as hair cells in the inner ear.

questions.text: The sensory neuron has pain-sensitive endings in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The hypothalamus acts as the body's smart control coordinating centre. This is not the correct option.
Bones are living tissues that make up the body's skeleton. This is not the correct option.
Sensory neurons have pain-sensitive endings in the skin, as skin is a sensory organ. This option is correct.
Muscles are tissues that help in the contraction and relaxation of the body. They are not sensory organs and therefore do not have sensory neurons.

questions.explanation: Sensory neurons have pain-sensitive endings in the skin, as the skin is a sensory organ. Sensory neurons are nerve cells that detect physical and chemical stimuli from the environment and inside the body, converting them into electrical signals called nerve impulses. These impulses are then transmitted to the central nervous system (CNS)—the brain and spinal cord—for processing, allowing for perceptions of touch, temperature, pain, light, sound, taste, and smell. Sensory neurons are found in sensory organs like the eyes, ears, skin, and nose, and play a crucial role in connecting the body to the brain.

questions.text: Which of the following statement about neuron is incorrect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: They not only conduct impulses but also generate them
options.explanation: Neurons are excitable cells, meaning they can produce electrical signals called action potentials in response to certain stimuli. The process by which neurons generate action potentials is known as "excitation" or "depolarization."

options.text: They are not the only cellular component of nervous system
options.explanation: While neurons are the primary functional cells in the nervous system, they are not the only cellular components. The nervous system is a complex network that consists of various types of cells, each playing a crucial role in its proper functioning.

options.text: They may show limited regenerative capabilities
options.explanation: Neurons, unlike many other types of cells in the body, generally have limited regenerative capabilities. The ability of neurons to regenerate and repair themselves after injury or damage varies depending on several factors, such as the type of neuron, the location of the injury, and the age of the individual.

options.text: Like all the living cell, when they mature and divide to form similar cells
options.explanation: Neurons, once they mature, typically do not divide to form similar cells through the process of cell division (mitosis). Unlike many other types of cells in the body, mature neurons generally lose their ability to undergo cell division and replicate themselves.

questions.explanation: Neurons, once they mature, generally do not divide to form similar cells through the process of mitosis, which is the typical cell division process in most other cell types. This limited ability to divide and replicate is known as "post-mitotic" or "amitotic" behavior. The reason for this limited division capability in mature neurons is related to their specialized structure and function. Neurons have a highly complex and specialized morphology, with extensive dendrites and axons that form intricate networks to transmit electrical signals and process information. Additionally, the connections between neurons, known as synapses, are critical for information transfer and neural communication.

questions.text: The neurons that interpret and receive information and stimulate motor neurons are what type of neuron?

options.explanation: Sensory neurons, also called afferent neurons, are responsible for transmitting sensory information from sensory receptors (such as eyes, ears, hands and feets) to the central nervous system. These neurons detect various stimuli, such as light, sound, touch, temperature, and pain, and convert them into electrical signals called action potentials.

Motor neurons are a specific type of neurons that play a critical role in the functioning of the nervous system. They are responsible for transmitting nerve impulses from the central nervous system (CNS) to the muscles, allowing for voluntary and involuntary movements.

Relay neurons, also known as interneurons or association neurons, are found entirely within the CNS and act as intermediaries between sensory neurons and motor neurons. They integrate and process incoming sensory information and facilitate communication between different parts of the brain and spinal cord.

The neurons that receive sensory input, interpret it, and pass signals to motor neurons are called interneurons (association neurons).They are found in the brain and spinal cord and form the link between sensory and motor pathways.

questions.explanation: Interneurons are essential components of the CNS, facilitating communication between sensory and motor neurons and playing a crucial role in information processing and coordinationof neural functions. Sensitivity is the capacity to detect and respond to stimuli, and nervous tissue is indeed sensitive. Nervous tissue contains specialized cells called sensory neurons that can detect different types of stimuli, such as light, sound, touch, temperature, and pain.

questions.text: An involuntary and nearly instantaneous movement in response to a stimulus is called:

questions.explanation: A reflex action is an automatic, involuntary, and rapid response to a stimulus, so this is the correct answer. A reflex is an automatic, involuntary, and rapid response to a stimulus that protects an organism from harm and aids survival. These responses occur through a reflex arc, a nerve pathway that bypasses the brain for instant reaction, though the signal is still sent to the brain for further processing or override. Examples include the sudden withdrawal from a hot surface, the pupil constricting in bright light, and the knee-jerk response to a tap.

questions.explanation: Myelin is a stack of specialized plasma membrane sheets produced by a glial cell that wraps itself around the axon. Thus, all electrical activities in axons are confined to the nodes of Ranvier, the sites where ions can flow across the axonal membrane.

questions.text: Which one of the following is common to all neurons?

Nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the myelin sheath that are found in myelinated neurons, allowing for faster signal transmission. However, not all neurons are myelinated, and thus, some do not have these nodes, making this option incorrect.

Myelin sheaths insulate axons and enhance the speed of impulse conduction. However, the thickness of the myelin sheath varies among neurons, and some are unmyelinated. Therefore, this characteristic is not universal among all neurons.

Schwann cells are responsible for the formation of the myelin sheath around peripheral nerves. While they play a critical role in myelination, not all neurons are associated with Schwann cells, particularly those in the central nervous system, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The cell body (soma) is the main nutritional and metabolic center of all neurons, containing the nucleus and responsible for biosynthesis of proteins and energy for neuronal function.

questions.text: All of the following are reflex actions EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Change in the size of the pupil in response to light
options.explanation: This is a reflex action known as the pupillary light reflex, where the pupil size adjusts involuntarily to regulate the amount of light entering the eye.

Swallowing is a complex process involving both voluntary and involuntary actions. Although the reflex action starts the process, the act of swallowing involves voluntary control, making it not a pure reflex action.

options.text: Sudden jerky withdrawal of hand when pricked
options.explanation: This action is a classic example of a reflex arc, where the body responds instantly and involuntarily to protect itself from harm by withdrawing the hand.

options.text: Knees jerk in response to a blow
options.explanation: This is known as the knee-jerk reflex, a sudden, involuntary response to a tap on the patellar tendon, demonstrating a reflex action.

questions.explanation: Reflex actions are involuntary and instantaneous responses to stimuli. The change in pupil size, jerky withdrawal of the hand, and knee jerk are all reflex actions because they occur automatically and without conscious thought. However, swallowing of the bolus, while it begins with a reflexive component, is primarily a voluntary action because it requires conscious control, especially after the initial trigger, making it not purely reflexive.

Peristalsis is an involuntary process controlled by the autonomic nervous system, specifically through the enteric nervous system, facilitating the movement of food through the digestive tract without conscious effort.

questions.text: Animals possess a nerve network or nervous system to respond to their environment; but the single-cell Amoeba does not possess any nerve cell. How does it come to know whether a particle it encounters is a grain of sand and not its food?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amoebas respond to chemical stimuli through chemotaxis, allowing them to differentiate between food and non-food particles based on chemical signals.
An Amoeba does not have skin or nerve cells that can detect particles; it relies solely on chemical signals for such determinations.
Hormones are not involved in the detection of particles by Amoebas; they use chemotaxis for this purpose.
This is incorrect as only chemotaxis is the mechanism used by Amoebas to identify particles.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that an Amoeba uses chemotaxis to determine whether a particle is food or not. Chemotaxis is a process by which an organism moves towards or away from chemical stimuli. In the case of an Amoeba, it detects chemical signals from potential food sources, enabling it to distinguish between edible and non-edible particles. The other options are incorrect because Amoebas do not possess skin or hormones to detect particles, nor do they use multiple mechanisms for this purpose.

questions.text: Metamorphosis in amphibians is under control of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sex hormones, such as estrogen and testosterone, are primarily involved in the development and regulation of the secondary sexual characteristics of an organism. While they play a crucial role in the overall development and maturation of an organism, they are not directly involved in the process of metamorphosis in amphibians.

The pituitary gland produces a variety of hormones that regulate various bodily functions, including growth and development. However, while pituitary hormones can influence growth and development, they are not the primary hormones responsible for controlling metamorphosis in amphibians.

The thyroid hormone is the primary hormone responsible for controlling metamorphosis in amphibians. It orchestrates the drastic changes that occur during the transition from the larval to adult stage of development in amphibians. This includes the resorption of certain larval organs and the differentiation of new adult organs.

The parathyroid hormone plays a crucial role in regulating calcium levels within the body. While calcium is essential for many bodily functions, including bone health, the parathyroid hormone does not directly control metamorphosis in amphibians.

questions.explanation: Thyroid hormones drive metamorphosis in amphibians, regulating resorption of larval organs and growth of adult organs.

questions.text: They detect pain and tissue damage:

options.explanation: Option A is not the correct answer. Baroreceptors are sensory receptors that detect changes in blood pressure. They are primarily found in the walls of blood vessels and the heart. Baroreceptors are not responsible for detecting pain and tissue damage.

Option B is not the correct answer. Chemoreceptors are sensory receptors that detect changes in chemical concentrations, such as the levels of oxygen, carbon dioxide, and pH in the blood. They are found in various organs, including the respiratory system and blood vessels. Chemoreceptors are not involved in detecting pain and tissue damage.

Option C is not the correct answer. Photoreceptors are sensory receptors that detect light and are primarily found in the eyes. They are responsible for vision and are not involved in detecting pain and tissue damage.

Option D is the correct answer. Nociceptors, also known as pain receptors, are sensory receptors that detect and respond to harmful or potentially damaging stimuli, such as tissue damage or extreme temperature. When activated, nociceptors transmit pain signals to the brain, alerting the body to potential harm.

questions.explanation: Correct. Nissl granules consist of rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) and ribosomes, which are essential for protein synthesis in neurons.

questions.text: Which of the following structures is present characteristically only in the mammalian brain?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Corpus fibrosum is related to ovarian structures and is not located in the brain. It forms in the ovary after the atresia of an ovarian follicle.
Although the corpus striatum is part of the brain, it is not exclusive to mammals; it is present in birds as well.
Corpus luteum is an endocrine gland in the ovary that secretes hormones, not a brain structure.
The corpus callosum is a large band of white matter that connects the right and left hemispheres of the brain in mammals, making it a distinctive feature of the mammalian brain.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the corpus callosum, which is a large band of white matter connecting the two hemispheres of the brain and is unique to mammals. This structure facilitates inter-hemispheric communication. Corpus fibrosum and corpus luteum are related to ovarian structures and have no connection to the brain, while corpus striatum is present in various animals, including birds, and is not exclusive to mammals.

questions.text: Nissl’s granules are found in the cyton of nerve cells. They have an affinity for basic dyes. The granules are made up of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mitochondria are known as the powerhouse of the cell, generating energy in the form of ATP, but they are not involved in Nissl's granules.
Cell metabolites are products of metabolism that can serve various functions, such as storage or signaling, but they do not constitute Nissl's granules.
Fat granules store energy in the form of lipids, but they are not related to Nissl's granules, which are involved in protein synthesis.
Nissl's granules consist of rough endoplasmic reticulum and clusters of ribosomes, which are essential for protein synthesis in nerve cells.

questions.explanation: Nissl's granules are prominently found in the cyton (cell body) of nerve cells, where they are involved in protein synthesis. They consist of rough endoplasmic reticulum and clusters of free ribosomes, which give them an affinity for basic dyes. This composition is crucial for the synthesis of proteins necessary for the neuron's functions.Why other options are incorrect:Mitochondria: While critical for energy production, they do not form Nissl's granules.Cell Metabolites: These are not structural components of Nissl's granules.Fat Granules: These are involved in energy storage, not protein synthesis.

questions.text: The opening at the base of the skull for spinal cord is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The foramen magnum is the large opening at the base of the skull through which the spinal cord passes and connects to the brain.
The foramen of Monro is a small conduit in the brain that connects the lateral ventricles to the third ventricle, unrelated to the spinal cord.
The obturator foramen is located in the pelvis, not the skull, and is not involved in the connection between the brain and spinal cord.

Also known as the median aperture, the foramen of Magendie is part of the ventricular system in the brain, linking the fourth ventricle and the cisterna magna, and does not relate to the spinal cord exit.

questions.explanation: The foramen magnum is the correct answer as it is the opening at the base of the skull that allows the spinal cord to connect with the brain. This connection is essential for the coordination and control of body functions. The other foramen options are located in different parts of the body or serve different functions. The foramen of Monro connects brain ventricles, the obturator foramen is in the pelvis, and the foramen of Magendie is part of the brain's ventricular system.

questions.text: The largest number of cell bodies of neurons in a human body is found in the —---
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brain is the central processing unit of the nervous system, containing the highest concentration of neurons and their cell bodies, also known as grey matter.
The spinal cord contains neuron cell bodies but is primarily responsible for transmitting signals between the brain and the rest of the body. It does not have as many neurons as the brain.
The retina is part of the eye and contains photoreceptor cells, not a large number of neuron cell bodies, unlike the brain.
The tongue has sensory neurons but lacks a significant concentration of neuron cell bodies compared to the brain.

questions.explanation: The brain is the major center of coordination and control in the human body, integrating sensory information and issuing commands. It contains the largest number of neuron cell bodies, known as grey matter, which is crucial for processing complex information. In contrast, the spinal cord serves as a communication pathway between the brain and the peripheral nerves, with fewer neuron cell bodies. The retina is specialized for detecting light, and the tongue primarily serves in taste sensation, neither of which have the extensive network of neuron cell bodies found in the brain.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about receptors?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Gustatory receptors detect taste while olfactory receptors detect smell.
options.explanation: This statement is accurate. Gustatory receptors on the tongue are responsible for detecting taste, while olfactory receptors in the nasal cavity detect odors.

options.text: Both gustatory and olfactory receptors detect smell.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Gustatory receptors are involved in taste detection, not smell.

options.text: Auditory receptors detect smell and olfactory receptors detect taste.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Auditory receptors are involved in hearing, and olfactory receptors are involved in detecting smells.

options.text: Olfactory receptors detect taste and gustatory receptors smell.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Olfactory receptors detect smells and gustatory receptors detect tastes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that gustatory receptors detect taste while olfactory receptors detect smell. Gustatory receptors are found in the taste buds on the tongue and are responsible for detecting different flavors. Olfactory receptors are located in the nasal cavity and detect airborne molecules associated with odors. The other options confuse the functions of these receptors, mixing up their specific roles or incorrectly assigning them to unrelated sensory functions such as hearing.

questions.text: The touch receptor organs of the somatosensory system include _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Somatosensory neurons are involved in transmitting sensory information but are not considered touch receptor organs themselves.

options.text: The skin, joint tissues, and muscles
options.explanation: While joint tissues and muscles are involved in proprioception, they are not the primary touch receptor organs. The skin is the main organ for detecting external touch stimuli.
These structures are more related to proprioception, which involves sensing the position and movement of the body rather than external touch stimuli.
The skin is the primary touch receptor organ, responsible for detecting external stimuli such as pressure, temperature changes, and pain, making it the correct option.

questions.explanation: While somatosensory neurons are found within specialised touch receptors, the touch receptor organ for the tactile system is the skin. These sensors will detect stimuli for pressure, temperature change, and pain. Though these sensations can be detected in some internal regions of the body as well, the sensation of touch must be triggered by an external stimulus. As such, the receptors must have contact with the external environment (via the skin).

questions.text: A typical neuron at rest:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Is more positive outside than inside
options.explanation: A typical neuron is more positive outside than inside due to the presence of Na+ K+ pump which countertransports 3Na+ outside and 2K+ inside, removing one extra positive charge outside every time movement occurs.

options.text: Is more negative outside than inside
options.explanation: At rest neuron is more positive outside

options.text: Has no charge on either side
options.explanation: Counter-transports of ions occurs in an unequal amount resulting in charges

options.text: Has an equal charge on either side
options.explanation: 3 Na+ move outside and 2K+ inside via sodium-potassium pump therefore unequal charge on either side.

questions.text: Neurilemma cells are also known as?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Special cells in the eye's retina that are responsible for converting light into signals that are sent to the brain. Photoreceptors give us our color vision and night vision. There are two types of photoreceptor cells: rods and cones. A number of eye problems can involve photoreceptor cell.

Odontoblasts form the dentine, a collagen-based mineralized tissue, through secretion of its collagenous and noncollagenous organic matrix components and by controlling the mineralization process. A conspicuous cell process arises from the cell body of odontoblasts and penetrates into the mineralized dentine.
A ganglion cyst is a fluid-filled swelling that usually develops near a joint or tendon. The cyst can range from the size of a pea to the size of a golf ball.

Neurilemma (also known as neurolemma, sheath of Schwann, or Schwann's sheath) is the outermost nucleated cytoplasmic layer of Schwann cells (also called neurolemmocytes) that surrounds the axon of the neuron. It forms the outermost layer of the nerve fiber in the peripheral nervous system.

questions.explanation: Schwann cell, also called the neurilemma cell, is any of the cells in the peripheral nervous system that produce the myelin sheath around neuronal axons.

questions.text: In which part of the nervous system will you find Schwann cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The central nervous system (CNS) is made up of the brain and spinal cord. It is one of 2 parts of the nervous system. The other part is the peripheral nervous system, which consists of nerves that connect the brain and spinal cord to the rest of the body. The central nervous system is the body's processing center.
Schwann cells (SCs) are the main glial cells of the peripheral nervous system which wrap around axons of motor and sensory neurons to form the myelin sheath.

The limbic system is the part of the brain involved in our behavioral and emotional responses, especially when it comes to behaviors we need for survival: feeding, reproduction and caring for our young, and fight or flight responses.

A sympathetic nervous system is a network of nerves that helps your body activate its “fight-or-flight” response. This system's activity increases when you're stressed, in danger or physically active.

questions.explanation: Schwann cells and satellite glial are the two main glial cell types of the peripheral nervous system (PNS). While satellite glia are found within ganglia in close association with neuronal somata, schwann cells are found in close contact with axons in the peripheral nerves.
